reasoning: The Lean formulation parameterizes all axis-aligned squares Y ⊆ X with at least one side on ∂X by their centers P ∈ X via the "max-square at P" construction: `room P = min(min a (1-a)) (min b (1-b))` is the L∞-distance to ∂X, and the resulting square always has a side on the boundary. Conversely, any Y satisfying (1)(2)(3) with center (a,b) and half-side s must satisfy s ≤ room(a,b) (from Y⊆X) and s ≥ min{a,1-a,b,1-b} (from one side touching ∂X), giving s = room(a,b). Thus there is a bijection between qualifying squares and centers P ∈ X (degenerate cases with room P=0 give trivial integrals). By Fubini for continuous f, the iterated intervalIntegral equals the double integral over the square. Though Lean quantifies over f : ℝ² → ℝ instead of f : X → ℝ, Tietze extension makes the two quantifications equivalent since hypothesis and conclusion only depend on f|X. The conclusion `∀ P ∈ X, f P = 0` correctly captures "f(x,y) = 0 for all x,y" on the domain X.
